mnemonics.title: SAFETY Properties
mnemonics.content: SAFE: S-tates cannot be violated, A-greement on single value, F-ault tolerant, E-very valid transaction included, T-wo-thirds majority, Y-ields consistent results

mnemonics.title: Consensus Types
mnemonics.content: POET: P-roof of W-ork, O-thers include E-veryone S-take, T-ake turns (PBFT/Raft)

mnemonics.title: PBFT Node Calculation
mnemonics.content: TOTAL = 3 x FAULTY + 1 (To tolerate f Byzantine faults, need 3f+1 total nodes)

mnemonics.title: Consensus Properties
mnemonics.content: SAL: SAFETY + AVAILABILITY + LIVENESS - the three pillars of distributed consensus

acronyms.usage: Consensus mechanism requiring computational work to add blocks
Consensus mechanism selecting validators based on token holdings
PoS with elected delegates validating transactions
Consensus algorithm tolerating Byzantine faults in distributed systems
Ability of system to tolerate nodes behaving arbitrarily (maliciously)
Impossibility result proving consensus difficulty in async systems

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a tree with branches - the valid chain is the longest branch from root to tip. Each new block extends one branch, and nodes always follow the longest branch.
Picture a ROUND TABLE with 4 nodes: Request → Pre-prepare (leader) → Prepare (all verify) → Commit (all agree) → Reply. The 3-phase handshake ensures agreement.
Think of miners as contestants in a lottery - everyone buys tickets (computes hashes) simultaneously, first to find winning number wins the block.
Two roads diverging - nodes pick the road with more markers (blocks/work), eventually everyone converges on the longer path.

analogies.analogy: Like ten army generals planning an attack, but some may be traitors sending conflicting orders. Loyal generals must agree on a unified plan despite the traitors.
Like a board of directors where those with more shares (stake) have more voting power, but if they act against company interests, they lose their shares.
Like gold mining - miners expend effort (energy) to find valuable blocks, making it costly to attack the network.
Like bank wire transfers vs blockchain: bank transfers can sometimes be reversed (probabilistic), while in-person cash is final (absolute).
